content.problemStatement:
For positive integers aaa and bbb, we say that a fraction ab\frac{a}{b}ba​ is good if it is equal to mm+1\frac{m}{m+1}m+1m​ for some positive integer mmm.

You are given an integer NNN. Find the number of pairs of integers i,ji, ji,j such that 1≤i,j≤N1 \le i, j \le N1≤i,j≤N and the fraction ii+1⋅j+1j\frac{i}{i+1} \cdot \frac{j+1}{j}i+1i​⋅jj+1​ is good.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T – the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains a single integer N (1 ≤ N ≤ 1,000,000).
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line with one integer – the number of ordered pairs (i, j) (1 ≤ i, j ≤ N) such that (i/(i+1))·((j+1)/j) equals m/(m+1) for some positive integer m.

solution.approach:
For a pair (i, j) let A = i·(j+1) and B = (i+1)·j. The fraction A/B equals m/(m+1) iff m = A/(B‑A) is a positive integer. Since B‑A = j‑i, the condition becomes:
  • j > i (otherwise m ≤ 0), and
  • (j‑i) divides i·(j+1).
Define d = j‑i (1 ≤ d ≤ N‑1) and write j = i + d. The condition turns into:
  d | i·(i+d+1).
Because i·(i+d+1) ≡ (i mod d)·((i+1) mod d) (mod d), let r = i mod d. The condition is
  r·(r+1) ≡ 0 (mod d).
Since r and r+1 are coprime, every prime power p^e dividing d must divide either r or r+1. Hence for each distinct prime power of d we have two independent choices:
  – r ≡ 0 (mod p^e)  or  – r ≡ -1 (mod p^e).
By the Chinese Remainder Theorem this yields exactly 2^{ω(d)} residues r (ω(d) = number of distinct prime factors of d) that satisfy the divisibility.
For each such residue r we count how many i (1 ≤ i ≤ N‑d) are congruent to r modulo d. The smallest i with residue r is:
  first = d   if r = 0,
  first = r   otherwise.
If first ≤ N‑d, the number of valid i is 1 + floor((N‑d‑first)/d).
Summing this over all residues for every d gives the answer.
Algorithm steps for each test case:
1. Build an SPF (smallest prime factor) array up to N using a linear sieve – O(N).
2. For each d from 1 to N‑1:
   a. Extract the distinct prime powers of d using the SPF array.
   b. Generate all residues r that satisfy r·(r+1) ≡ 0 (mod d) by a recursive CRT combination of the two possibilities (0 or -1) for each prime power. The number of residues never exceeds 2^{ω(d)} ≤ 128 for N ≤ 10^6.
   c. For each residue compute the contribution of i as described and add to the total.
3. Output the total.
The overall complexity is O(N log log N) for the sieve plus Σ_d 2^{ω(d)} which is well below 2·10^7 for N = 10^6, easily fitting into the time limit. Memory consumption is O(N) for the SPF array.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N log log N) per test case (dominated by the linear sieve and the small‑constant work per d).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) – the SPF array and a few auxiliary vectors.
